C:
Use Postgres. For an early B2B SaaS primary datastore, it's the right default, and the bar for choosing MongoDB instead is high enough that most early-stage teams won't clear it.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B data is relational, and you can't predict the joins you'll need yet.** Accounts have users, users have roles, roles have permissions, subscriptions tie to plans, usage ties to billing. That's a graph of foreign-key relationships, and B2B specifically piles on multi-tenancy, org hierarchies, audit trails, and per-customer configuration. Postgres models this natively with joins and constraints. In MongoDB you either denormalize (and then fight data drift when the same fact lives in three documents) or do application-side joins (and then you've rebuilt half a relational engine by hand, worse).

**Early-stage means your schema is wrong and will change often — and that's an argument *for* Postgres, not against it.** The common pitch for Mongo is "schemaless, so I can iterate fast." In practice the schema doesn't disappear; it just moves from the database into scattered, unenforced assumptions in your application code. When you're moving fast and breaking things, an enforced schema with real migrations (and tools like a migration framework) is what stops a careless change from silently corrupting a customer's data. Postgres also has `JSONB`, so for the genuinely unstructured or fast-moving parts you get document-style flexibility *inside* a relational store — you don't have to choose up front.

**Transactions and integrity are non-negotiable for B2B.** You're handling billing, entitlements, and seat counts where money and access depend on correctness. Postgres gives you mature multi-row ACID transactions and foreign-key/uniqueness constraints enforced by the database. Mongo has added multi-document transactions, but they're a more recent and less central part of the model, and you'll lean on them constantly in this domain.

**Operationally it's the safer bet.** Postgres runs as a managed service everywhere (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, etc.), the hiring pool knows SQL, and the analytics/BI ecosystem assumes SQL — which matters the first time a customer or your own team asks for a report you didn't anticipate. It scales comfortably well past where an early B2B SaaS will be for years.

**When MongoDB would actually be the better call:** your core data is genuinely document-shaped and schema varies per record with few cross-entity relationships (event logs, CMS content, product catalogs with wildly heterogeneous attributes, IoT/telemetry); write throughput and horizontal sharding are your dominant constraint from day one; or the team has deep Mongo operational experience and none with SQL. For a typical early B2B SaaS, none of these usually hold.

Net: start on Postgres, reach for `JSONB` where you need document flexibility, and only adopt MongoDB later for a specific workload that genuinely fits the document model — as a complement, not the primary store.
T:
Use Postgres. For an early B2B SaaS primary datastore, it's the right default, and the bar for choosing MongoDB instead is high enough that most early-stage teams won't clear it.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B data is relational, and you can't predict the joins you'll need yet.** Accounts have users, users have roles, roles have permissions, subscriptions tie to plans, usage ties to billing. That's a graph of foreign-key relationships, and B2B specifically piles on multi-tenancy, org hierarchies, audit trails, and per-customer configuration. Postgres models this natively with joins and constraints. In MongoDB you either denormalize (and then fight data drift when the same fact lives in three documents) or do application-side joins (and then you've rebuilt half a relational engine by hand, worse).

**Early-stage means your schema is wrong and will change often — and that's an argument *for* Postgres, not against it.** The common pitch for Mongo is "schemaless, so I can iterate fast." In practice the schema doesn't disappear; it just moves from the database into scattered, unenforced assumptions in your application code. When you're moving fast and breaking things, an enforced schema with real migrations (and tools like a migration framework) is what stops a careless change from silently corrupting a customer's data. Postgres also has `JSONB`, so for the genuinely unstructured or fast-moving parts you get document-style flexibility *inside* a relational store — you don't have to choose up front.

**Transactions and integrity are non-negotiable for B2B.** You're handling billing, entitlements, and seat counts where money and access depend on correctness. Postgres gives you mature multi-row ACID transactions and foreign-key/uniqueness constraints enforced by the database. Mongo has added multi-document transactions, but they're a more recent and less central part of the model, and you'll lean on them constantly in this domain.

**Operationally it's the safer bet.** Postgres runs as a managed service everywhere (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, etc.), the hiring pool knows SQL, and the analytics/BI ecosystem assumes SQL — which matters the first time a customer or your own team asks for a report you didn't anticipate. It scales comfortably well past where an early B2B SaaS will be for years.

**When MongoDB would actually be the better call:** your core data is genuinely document-shaped and schema varies per record with few cross-entity relationships (event logs, CMS content, product catalogs with wildly heterogeneous attributes, IoT/telemetry); write throughput and horizontal sharding are your dominant constraint from day one; or the team has deep Mongo operational experience and none with SQL. For a typical early B2B SaaS, none of these usually hold.

**What would change this recommendation.** This is a default, not a universal law — it rests on the assumption that your data is relationship-heavy, correctness-sensitive, and modest in scale early on. Flip those assumptions and the answer flips: if your actual core workload turns out to be high-volume document or telemetry data with few cross-entity joins, if horizontal write-scaling becomes a day-one constraint rather than a someday-problem, or if your team genuinely has deep Mongo expertise and none with SQL, then MongoDB becomes the reasonable primary store. The main limitation to flag honestly: I'm reasoning from the typical shape of early B2B SaaS, not from your specific schema and access patterns — if those diverge from the picture above, re-weigh accordingly. The fastest way to disconfirm the Postgres default is to sketch your three or four highest-volume queries and your core entities; if they're mostly self-contained documents rather than joins across tenants, users, plans, and usage, that's the signal to reconsider.

Net: start on Postgres, reach for `JSONB` where you need document flexibility, and only adopt MongoDB later for a specific workload that genuinely fits the document model — as a complement, not the primary store.
P:
Use Postgres. For an early B2B SaaS primary datastore it's the right default, and the bar to justify MongoDB instead is high enough that most early-stage teams won't clear it.

The reasoning:

**B2B data is relational, and you can't yet predict the joins you'll need.** Accounts have users, users have roles, roles have permissions, subscriptions map to plans, usage rolls up to billing. On top of that base graph, B2B piles on multi-tenancy, org hierarchies, audit trails, and per-customer configuration. Postgres models all of this natively with joins and foreign keys. In MongoDB you either denormalize — and then fight data drift when the same fact lives in three documents — or do joins in application code, which is rebuilding a relational engine by hand, worse. The trap is that the relationships you haven't discovered yet are exactly the ones the document model makes expensive to add later.

**Your schema is wrong and will change often — that's an argument *for* Postgres, not against it.** The pitch for Mongo is "schemaless, iterate fast." But the schema never actually disappears; it just moves out of the database and into scattered, unenforced assumptions spread across your application code. When you're moving fast, an enforced schema with real, reviewable migrations is what stops a careless change from silently corrupting a customer's data. And for the parts that genuinely are unstructured or fast-moving, Postgres `JSONB` gives you document-style flexibility inside the relational store — so you don't have to pick one model up front.

**Transactions and integrity are non-negotiable here.** You're handling billing, entitlements, and seat counts, where money and access depend on getting writes exactly right. Postgres gives you mature multi-row ACID transactions plus uniqueness and foreign-key constraints enforced by the database itself, not hoped for in application code. Mongo does support multi-document transactions now, but the document model is designed to avoid them by embedding related data in a single document — and B2B's cross-entity relationships are precisely what defeats that design, pushing you onto the transaction path constantly, where it's less idiomatic and carries more caveats.

**Operationally it's the safer bet.** Postgres is a first-class managed service everywhere (RDS/Aurora, Cloud SQL, Supabase, Neon, and others), the hiring pool already knows SQL, and the analytics and BI ecosystem assumes SQL — which you'll feel the first time a customer or your own team wants a report you didn't design for. It scales comfortably past where an early B2B SaaS will be for years.

**When MongoDB is genuinely the better call:** your core data is document-shaped, with schema that varies per record and few cross-entity relationships (event logs, CMS content, catalogs with wildly heterogeneous attributes, IoT/telemetry); horizontal write throughput and sharding are your dominant constraint from day one; or the team has deep Mongo operations experience and none with SQL. For a typical early B2B SaaS, none of these usually holds.

**The deciding factor is the asymmetry of regret.** If you start on Postgres and later hit a true document workload, you add Mongo (or just a `JSONB` table) for that one slice — cheap and contained. If you start on Mongo and discover, as B2B teams reliably do, that your data was relational all along, you're re-platforming the core of your product under load. Bet on the mistake that's easy to walk back.

Net: start on Postgres, use `JSONB` where you need document flexibility, and adopt MongoDB later only for a specific workload that genuinely fits the document model — as a complement, not the primary store.
